article.title: Apple Plans to Replace Core ML with Core AI Framework at WWDC 2026, Signaling a Developer-Facing AI Platform Shift
article.summary: Apple will retire Core ML at WWDC 2026 and introduce Core AI, a modernized framework for integrating large language models and generative AI into iOS 27 apps.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Apple is preparing to retire Core ML, the machine learning framework it introduced in 2017, and replace it with a new system called Core AI at its Worldwide Developers Conference in June 2026, according to reporting by [9to5Mac](https://9to5mac.com/2026/03/01/apple-replacing-core-ml-with-modernized-core-ai-framework-for-ios-27-at-wwdc/) citing Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The transition, expected to accompany iOS 27, marks a structural change in how Apple exposes artificial intelligence capabilities to third-party developers.

## From Machine Learning to Generative AI

Core ML, launched in 2017, was built around traditional machine learning tasks — image classification, natural language processing, sound recognition — and allowed developers to bundle trained models directly into their apps. The framework served its purpose for a generation of on-device ML features, but as the industry pivoted toward large language models and generative pipelines, the tooling began to show its age.

According to [Gurman's reporting cited by 9to5Mac](https://9to5mac.com/2026/03/01/apple-replacing-core-ml-with-modernized-core-ai-framework-for-ios-27-at-wwdc/), Apple recognizes that "machine learning" is now a dated term that no longer resonates with developers or consumers. Core AI is intended to serve the same foundational role — "helping developers integrate outside AI models into their apps" — while expanding scope to cover LLM inference, multimodal processing, and generative pipelines.

[AppleMagazine](https://applemagazine.com/apple-core-ai/) reports that the framework will support on-device large language model inference, multimodal inputs spanning text, image, and audio, and generative workflows including conversational systems. Crucially, Core AI is expected to provide native access to Apple Foundation Models, the company's own large-scale AI systems trained internally.

## Hardware and Developer Implications

The framework is designed to take direct advantage of Apple silicon's Neural Engine and unified memory architecture. [AppleMagazine notes](https://applemagazine.com/apple-core-ai/) that iPhone 17 Pro models, with enhanced processing headroom, stand to benefit most from Core AI's on-device inference capabilities.

For developers, the change reduces integration complexity. Rather than managing external API calls separately, applications will be able to rely on system-level services exposed through Core AI. Apple is also said to be exploring the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as a possible approach to connecting third-party AI models, a sign that the company is watching the emerging open standard for AI tool interoperability, according to [iLounge](https://www.ilounge.com/news/apple-moving-to-core-ai-replacing-core-ml-in-a-wwdc-2026-reveal).

Apple's standard approach of maintaining migration pathways suggests both frameworks may coexist during a transition period, though Core AI is positioned as the primary target for new development.

## Broader AI Strategy at WWDC 2026

Core AI will not be the only AI headline at this year's WWDC. Apple has been working on a significant Siri overhaul, codenamed Campos, that will turn the assistant into a conversational chatbot running on Google Gemini models, according to [MacRumors](https://www.macrumors.com/2026/01/25/siri-google-gemini-release-date/). A partial rollout of Gemini-powered Siri capabilities was already expected as early as iOS 26.5, with deeper integration arriving in iOS 27.

Taken together, Core AI and the Siri overhaul represent two layers of Apple's response to the generative AI era: one facing consumers, the other facing developers. Core AI provides the plumbing; the revamped Siri provides the surface experience. Both converge on a broader ambition to position AI as a platform layer embedded throughout iOS rather than a set of discrete, fragmented features.

## Context and Caveats

All current reporting on Core AI traces to Bloomberg's Gurman, whose track record on Apple pre-announcements is strong but not infallible. Apple has not officially confirmed any of these plans. WWDC 2026 is expected in June, and the company typically maintains a strict embargo on developer framework details until the keynote.

The significance of the shift, if it proceeds as described, extends beyond branding. Renaming the framework from "ML" to "AI" reflects how fundamentally the target use case has changed: from bundling small, task-specific models into apps to providing a system-wide runtime for large, general-purpose models running on-device. That change, if Apple executes it well, could give iOS a meaningful edge in the on-device AI application space at a time when competitors are working to close the gap on Apple silicon's efficiency advantages.
